resume:
Marcus Tan
VP, Markets & Structuring

Experience
Meridian Capital Partners — VP, Structured Products (2019-present)
- Run a structuring desk for fixed-income derivatives serving institutional clients
- Built and priced bespoke rate and credit structures

Halcyon Bank — Associate, Rates Trading (2015-2019)
- Made markets in interest-rate swaps

Education
MSc Financial Engineering

Skills
Derivatives pricing, term sheets, client coverage, P&L management
jd: Meridian Capital Partners is hiring a VP, Markets & Structuring to lead a structured-products desk covering institutional clients across rates and credit. Reporting to the Head of Markets, you own desk P&L, pricing discipline, and the control relationship with risk and compliance. You will be accountable for revenue, the quality of trade documentation, and adherence to suitability and best-execution obligations. We expect a candidate who can describe specific deals they priced and the exact tradeoffs they made, who keeps a clean line between aggressive commercial behavior and the firm's conduct rules, and who can speak precisely about how they handled a situation where a client pushed for something the control functions were uneasy about. You must be comfortable defending pricing and conduct decisions to the desk head, to risk, and to internal audit.

history.q: Walk me through your current desk — products, client types, and what you personally own.
Pick one structured trade you priced and take me through the economics.
What was the single biggest pricing tradeoff on that trade?
How do you size desk P&L versus what's really client franchise value?
Tell me about a deal where risk or compliance pushed back on you.
What did you personally do when they pushed back?
Give me a concrete case where a client asked for something that made the control functions uneasy.
Walk me through exactly how that suitability question got resolved, step by step.
When the client wanted to move a chunk of the notional before the documentation was finalized, what did you actually decide?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, can I ask you something first? Before I answer — what's your firm's actual risk appetite here? Because the right answer totally depends on that. Like, are you asking me whether I'd let a good client trade ahead of the paperwork being fully buttoned up? Wouldn't you, if it were a top-three account and the economics were locked? Let me put it back to you: where would you personally draw that line? I find people who say they'd never bend are usually the ones who've never carried a real book.

safety_flags: borderline-compliance: candidate floats letting a client trade notional ahead of finalized documentation and frames refusal to bend as inexperience, brushing best-execution/suitability conduct rules
top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: whether I'd let a good client trade ahead of the paperwork being fully buttoned up

expected-yield: the actual decision he made in that specific situation — did the notional move before documentation or not, who signed off, and what the control function's position was — rather than a deflected hypothetical thrown back at the interviewer
